verses.flow: The vision of Isaiah the son of Amoz, which he saw concerning Judah and Jerusalem in the days of Uzziah, Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah, kings of Judah.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Isaiah's ministry spans four kings (~740-686 BC), making it one of the longest prophetic careers in the OT. The Hebrew 'chazon' (vision) implies not a single event but a collected body of revelation. Isaiah's name means "the LORD saves" (Yesha'yahu), which encapsulates the entire book's message: judgment is real, but salvation is the LORD's final word.

verses.flow: "What is the multitude of your sacrifices to Me?" says the LORD. "I have had enough of burnt offerings of rams and the fat of well-fed beasts, and in the blood of bulls and lambs and goats I take no delight.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: God does not reject sacrifice per se (He commanded it) but sacrifice divorced from obedience and justice. Samuel made the same point (1 Sam 15:22). This passage anticipates the prophetic critique that culminates in Christ's temple cleansing (Matt 21:12-13). The Hebrew 'lo chafatzti' (I take no delight) is devastating -- the sacrificial system, given by God Himself, becomes repulsive when performed by unrepentant hands.

verses.flow: "Come now, and let us reason together," says the LORD. "Though your sins are as scarlet, they shall be white as snow; though they are red like crimson, they shall become like wool.
verses.note: MESSIANIC: This is one of the most beloved verses in Isaiah. The Hebrew uses two different words for red: 'shanim' (scarlet, the dye) and 'tola' (crimson, from the crushed worm). The promise of whitening -- snow and wool -- anticipates the complete cleansing that only God can provide. The NT fulfillment is explicit: "the blood of Jesus His Son cleanses us from all sin" (1 John 1:7). The invitation "let us reason together" (Hebrew: 'niwwakchah') is a legal term -- God invites His accused people to a settlement.

verses.flow: And it shall come to pass in the last days that the mountain of the house of the LORD shall be established as the chief of the mountains, and shall be lifted up above the hills; and all the nations shall stream to it.
verses.note: MESSIANIC: "In the last days" (Hebrew: 'be'acharit hayyamim') is a technical eschatological phrase. This prophecy appears nearly identically in Micah 4:1-3, suggesting both drew from a common prophetic tradition or one quoted the other. The "mountain of the house of the LORD" being "established as the chief" (Hebrew: 'be'rosh', literally "as the head") describes Zion's exaltation above all earthly powers. The nations "streaming" ('naharu') to it reverses Babel (Gen 11) -- instead of mankind building up to God, God draws all nations to Himself.

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse serves as the chapter's conclusion and thesis: human authority is nothing before God. The Hebrew 'neshamah' (breath) in his nostrils recalls Gen 2:7 -- man's life is entirely dependent on God's sustaining breath. This is a rebuke of all human pride and a call to trust God alone.

verses.flow: For behold, the Lord, the LORD of hosts, is taking away from Jerusalem and from Judah support and supply, every support of bread and every support of water;
verses.note: HEBREW: The phrase 'mash'en u-mash'enah' (support and supply) uses both masculine and feminine forms to indicate totality -- every kind of support. God systematically removes every pillar of society: military (v.2), judicial, prophetic, civic, and economic. The catalog of removed leaders (vv.2-3) is one of the most comprehensive in the prophets.

For Jerusalem has stumbled, and Judah has fallen, because their tongue and their deeds are against the LORD, to rebel against the eyes of His glory.
The expression of their faces testifies against them, and they declare their sin like Sodom; they do not hide it. Woe to their soul! For they have brought evil upon themselves.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "They declare their sin like Sodom; they do not hide it." Open, brazen sin is a marker of a society in terminal decline. When shame disappears, judgment is near. The comparison to Sodom is not merely about specific sins but about the posture of defiance -- the refusal to even acknowledge wrongdoing.

verses.flow: Say to the righteous that it shall be well with him, for they shall eat the fruit of their deeds.
verses.note: MESSIANIC: "Say to the righteous that it shall be well with him" -- even in the midst of corporate judgment, God distinguishes the righteous from the wicked. This is the seed of the remnant theology that Isaiah develops throughout the book. The righteous "eat the fruit of their deeds" -- not merit-based salvation, but the principle that faithfulness bears fruit (Gal 6:7-9).

verses.flow: Woe to the wicked! It shall be ill with him, for what his hands have done shall be done to him.

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The vineyard metaphor here anticipates chapter 5's Song of the Vineyard. The elders and princes are charged with devouring what they were meant to tend. Jesus draws directly on this imagery in the parable of the wicked tenants (Matt 21:33-41). The Hebrew 'ge'altem' (you have devoured) carries the sense of consuming by fire -- total destruction of what was entrusted.

verses.note: MESSIANIC: "The Branch of the LORD" (Hebrew: 'tsemach YHWH') is a key messianic title that recurs in Jeremiah 23:5, 33:15 and Zechariah 3:8, 6:12. The word 'tsemach' means "sprout" or "growth" -- something organic emerging from apparent death (cf. 11:1, the shoot from Jesse's stump). The dual description -- "Branch of the LORD" (divine origin) and "fruit of the land" (human origin) -- prefigures the Messiah's dual nature. He is both from God and from the land of Israel. The "survivors of Israel" ('peletat Yisrael') connects to the remnant theology that pervades Isaiah.

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The fifth woe targets moral inversion -- calling evil good and good evil. This is the deepest form of corruption: not merely doing evil but redefining it as virtue. Isaiah identifies this as the hallmark of a society beyond correction. Paul echoes this in Romans 1:32 -- those who "not only do them but give approval to those who practice them."

verses.flow: In the year that King Uzziah died, I saw the Lord sitting upon a throne, high and lifted up; and the train of His robe filled the temple.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Uzziah's death (c. 740 BC) ended a prosperous reign and created a political vacuum. At this moment of national crisis, Isaiah sees the true King. The Hebrew 'Adonai' (the Lord, sovereign master) emphasizes God's absolute authority. "High and lifted up" ('ram ve-nissa') -- the same language used of the Servant in Isa 52:13. John 12:41 explicitly states that Isaiah "saw His glory and spoke of Him," identifying the one on the throne as the pre-incarnate Christ.

verses.flow: Seraphim were standing above Him; each one had six wings: with two he covered his face, and with two he covered his feet, and with two he flew.
verses.note: HEBREW: 'Seraphim' (literally "burning ones") appear only here in Scripture. The six-winged form is unique to this passage. Covering the face suggests that even the highest angelic beings cannot bear the full glory of God. Covering the feet is a posture of humility and reverence (feet often a euphemism for the whole body's vulnerability). The two wings for flying indicate readiness for immediate service. These are divine council beings -- members of God's heavenly court (cf. 1 Kings 22:19; Job 1:6; Ps 82:1).

verses.flow: And one called to another and said, "Holy, holy, holy is the LORD of hosts; the whole earth is full of His glory!"
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The Trisagion -- "Holy, holy, holy" ('qadosh, qadosh, qadosh') -- is the ONLY place in the OT where an attribute of God is repeated three times. Hebrew uses repetition for emphasis: "holy" means set apart; "holy, holy" means supremely set apart; "holy, holy, holy" is the superlative of superlatives -- absolute, infinite holiness beyond human comprehension. Early church fathers (and Rev 4:8, which echoes this passage exactly) saw the threefold repetition as a veiled reference to the Trinity. "The whole earth is full of His glory" ('melo kol ha'arets kevodo') -- God's glory is not confined to the temple but fills all creation.

verses.note: HEBREW: "The foundations of the thresholds shook" -- the Hebrew 'ammot ha-sippim' refers to the door-pivot sockets in the temple threshold. The physical structure cannot contain God's holiness. Smoke filling the house recalls the glory cloud at Sinai (Exod 19:18) and Solomon's temple dedication (1 Kings 8:10-11). God's presence is simultaneously glorious and terrifying.

verses.flow: And I said, "Woe is me! For I am undone, for I am a man of unclean lips, and I dwell in the midst of a people of unclean lips; for my eyes have seen the King, the LORD of hosts!"
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Isaiah's response to the Holy is immediate undoing. The Hebrew 'nidmeti' can mean "I am ruined" or "I am silenced" -- both senses apply. Encounter with the living God produces not self-confidence but devastating self-awareness. "Unclean lips" -- speech is the organ of prophecy; Isaiah recognizes his unfitness for the prophetic call before it is given. Every genuine call in Scripture follows this pattern: Moses (Exod 3:11), Jeremiah (Jer 1:6), Peter (Luke 5:8).

The declaration "your iniquity is taken away" ('avonekha sar') uses passive voice -- Isaiah does nothing; God does everything. This is pure grace: atonement precedes commission.

verses.flow: And I heard the voice of the Lord saying, "Whom shall I send, and who will go for Us?" Then I said, "Here am I! Send me."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Whom shall I send, and who will go for Us?" The shift from singular ("I") to plural ("Us") has generated extensive theological discussion. The plural echoes Gen 1:26 ("Let Us make man") and Gen 11:7 ("Let Us go down"). In context, this is the divine council deliberation -- God speaks among His heavenly court (cf. 1 Kings 22:19-22). Christian theology sees here a Trinitarian hint: the one God who is plural in personhood. Isaiah's response -- "Here am I! Send me" ('hineni shelacheni') -- is among the most famous volunteer statements in all of Scripture.

verses.note: MESSIANIC: This judicial hardening prophecy is the most-quoted Isaiah passage in the NT. Jesus cites it in Matt 13:14-15 to explain why He teaches in parables. Paul cites it as his final word to the Roman Jews in Acts 28:26-27. John cites it in John 12:40. The pattern is devastating: hearing without understanding, seeing without perceiving. God judicially confirms the people in the condition they have chosen.

verses.flow: Make the heart of this people dull, and their ears heavy, and shut their eyes; lest they see with their eyes, and hear with their ears, and understand with their heart, and turn and be healed."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Make the heart of this people dull" -- the Hebrew imperatives ('hashmen', 'hakbed', 'hasha') mean "make fat," "make heavy," "smear shut." God commands Isaiah to preach a message that will harden rather than convert. This is judicial hardening: God giving people over to what they have already chosen. The purpose clause "lest they... turn and be healed" indicates that the hardening is temporary and purposeful -- it serves God's larger redemptive plan (cf. Rom 11:25-26, "a partial hardening has come upon Israel, until the fullness of the Gentiles has come in").

verses.note: HEBREW: "If you will not believe, surely you shall not be established." This is one of the great wordplays in Isaiah: 'im lo ta'aminu ki lo te'amenu' -- the verbs 'believe' and 'be established' share the same root ('aman'), from which we get "Amen." Faith and stability are linguistically and theologically linked: without trust in God, there is no standing firm. Ahaz's refusal to believe will lead to Judah's destabilization.

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: God invites Ahaz to ask for any sign -- "deep as Sheol or high as heaven." The scope is unlimited. verses.flow: Therefore the Lord Himself will give you a sign: Behold, the virgin shall conceive and bear a son, and shall call his name Immanuel.
verses.note: MESSIANIC: This is the most debated verse in Isaiah and one of the most important messianic prophecies in the OT. The Hebrew word 'almah' means "young woman of marriageable age" -- in every OT usage (Gen 24:43; Exod 2:8; Ps 68:25; Prov 30:19; Song 1:3, 6:8) it refers to a young, unmarried woman, implying virginity by social context. The more explicit term 'bethulah' specifically denotes virginity, but 'almah' carries the nuance of youth and hiddenness. Critically, the LXX translators (c. 250 BC), Jewish scholars themselves, chose 'parthenos' (virgin) to render 'almah' -- centuries before Christianity. Matthew 1:22-23 cites this as fulfilled in Mary's virginal conception: "Behold, the virgin shall conceive and bear a son, and they shall call his name Immanuel, which means God with us." The name 'Immanu-El' -- "God with us" -- is itself a staggering theological claim: this child somehow embodies God's presence. The definite article "THE virgin" ('ha-almah') points to a specific, known figure, not just any young woman. The immediate context involves a sign to Ahaz during the Syro-Ephraimite crisis (735 BC), but the grandeur of the prophecy transcends any near fulfillment. Isaiah 8:8 calls the land "your land, O Immanuel" -- ascribing land ownership to this figure, which is a divine prerogative.

verses.note: HEBREW: "The waters of Shiloah that flow gently" -- Shiloah (Siloam) was a modest stream feeding Jerusalem's water supply, symbolizing God's quiet, sufficient provision through the Davidic dynasty. And they will look to the earth, but behold, distress and darkness, the gloom of anguish. And they will be thrust into thick darkness.

verses.flow: But there will be no gloom for her who was in anguish. In the former time He brought into contempt the land of Zebulun and the land of Naphtali, but in the latter time He has made glorious the way of the sea, the land beyond the Jordan, Galilee of the nations.
verses.note: MESSIANIC: This verse (often numbered as 9:1 in Christian Bibles but 8:23 in the Hebrew MT) is directly quoted in Matt 4:14-16 as fulfilled when Jesus began His ministry in Galilee. "Galilee of the nations" ('Galil ha-goyim') -- the very region considered least significant becomes the theater of Messiah's appearing. The movement from darkness to light anticipates 9:1-2.

verses.flow: The people who walked in darkness have seen a great light; those who dwelt in a land of deep darkness, on them has light shone.
verses.note: MESSIANIC: "The people who walked in darkness have seen a great light." Matthew 4:14-16 quotes this as fulfilled when Jesus moved His ministry base to Capernaum in Galilee. The Hebrew 'or gadol' (great light) echoes the creation of light in Gen 1:3 -- Messiah's coming is a new creation event. The "land of deep darkness" ('tsalmawet', literally "shadow of death") is the same word used in Psalm 23:4.

words.m: preposition + verb piel infinitive construct + suffix

verses.flow: You have multiplied the nation; You have increased its joy. They rejoice before You as with the joy at harvest, as they are glad when they divide the spoil.
For the yoke of his burden, and the staff on his shoulder, the rod of his oppressor, You have broken as on the day of Midian.
verses.note: HEBREW: "The day of Midian" ('ke-yom Midyan') refers to Gideon's miraculous victory over Midian with only 300 men (Judg 7). The allusion is deliberate: just as God delivered Israel through absurdly small means, so the messianic deliverance will not come by human military power but by divine intervention.

verses.flow: For every boot of the tramping warrior in battle tumult and every garment rolled in blood will be burned as fuel for the fire.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Every boot of the tramping warrior... will be burned as fuel for the fire." The implements of war are destroyed, not repurposed. This goes beyond "swords into plowshares" (2:4) -- the very materials of warfare are consumed. The messianic kingdom is so peaceful that weapons become unthinkable.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd fem sing
conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: For a child will be born to us, a son will be given to us; and the government will rest on His shoulders; and His name will be called Wonderful Counselor, Mighty God, Eternal Father, Prince of Peace.
verses.note: MESSIANIC: This is THE premier messianic prophecy in the OT. "For a child will be born to us" -- fully human. "A son will be given to us" -- the passive "given" implies divine origin; the Son is a gift from above (cf. John 3:16). "The government will rest on His shoulders" -- sovereign authority. The FOUR NAMES are extraordinary: (1) 'Pele Yo'ets' -- "Wonderful Counselor": 'pele' means supernatural wonder, used of God's acts (Exod 15:11; Judg 13:18); (2) 'El Gibbor' -- "Mighty God": this is an unambiguous deity title. 'El' is God; 'Gibbor' is mighty warrior. Isaiah uses this exact phrase for YHWH in 10:21. There is no legitimate way to reduce this to "mighty hero" -- it is a claim of deity for the child; (3) 'Avi-Ad' -- "Father of Eternity" or "Eternal Father": not that Messiah IS the Father (Trinitarian distinction applies) but that He is the source and author of eternal life, the everlasting protector; (4) 'Sar Shalom' -- "Prince of Peace": the one who establishes ultimate peace (shalom = wholeness, completeness, nothing broken, nothing missing).

words.m: conjunction + preposition + verb qal infinitive construct + suffix

verses.flow: Of the increase of His government and of peace there will be no end, on the throne of David and over His kingdom, to establish it and to uphold it with justice and with righteousness, from this time forth and forevermore. The zeal of the LORD of hosts will do this.
verses.note: MESSIANIC: "On the throne of David" -- this places the child of v.5 squarely in the Davidic covenant (2 Sam 7:12-16). "From this time forth and forevermore" ('me-attah ve-ad olam') -- the reign is eternal, which no human king could fulfill. "The zeal of the LORD of hosts will do this" -- the accomplishment is entirely God's initiative. This verse is the answer to every doubt: God Himself guarantees it.

verses.flow: The Lord has sent a word against Jacob, and it will fall on Israel;
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The "refrain of judgment" section begins here (9:7-10:4), marked by the repeated phrase "For all this His anger has not turned away, and His hand is still stretched out" (vv. 11, 16, 20; 10:4). This fourfold repetition creates a drumbeat of unrelenting judgment against unrepentant Israel.

verses.flow: and all the people will know, Ephraim and the inhabitants of Samaria, who say in pride and in arrogance of heart:
"The bricks have fallen, but we will build with hewn stones; the sycamores have been cut down, but we will put cedars in their place."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "The bricks have fallen, but we will build with hewn stones" -- this is the pride of self-reliance. Instead of repentance after judgment, the people double down: they will rebuild bigger and better. It is the attitude of defiance rather than contrition -- upgrading the materials while ignoring the Builder. Some commentators note that this verse was quoted after national tragedies as a pledge of resilience, missing Isaiah's point entirely: the correct response is not rebuilding but repenting.

verses.flow: to turn aside the needy from justice and to rob the poor of My people of their right, that widows may be their spoil, and that they may plunder the fatherless!
What will you do on the day of punishment, in the ruin that will come from afar? To whom will you flee for help, and where will you leave your wealth?
Nothing remains but to crouch among the prisoners or to fall among the slain. For all this His anger has not turned away, and His hand is still stretched out.
Woe to Assyria, the rod of My anger; the staff in their hands is My fury!
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Woe to Assyria, the rod of My anger" -- God uses pagan empires as instruments of His judgment. Assyria does not know it is serving God's purpose (v.7), yet God holds Assyria accountable for its arrogance (v.12). This is one of the clearest statements of divine sovereignty and human responsibility in the OT. God ordains the means; the agents remain morally culpable for their intentions.

verses.flow: Shall the axe boast over him who hews with it, or the saw magnify itself against him who wields it? As if a rod should wave him who lifts it, or as if a staff should lift up what is not wood!
verses.note: HEBREW: The rhetorical questions are devastating: "Shall the axe boast over him who hews with it?" The Hebrew 'hit-pa'er' (to glorify oneself) exposes the absurdity of the instrument claiming credit for the craftsman's work. Assyria is a tool in God's hand, nothing more. This theology of instruments applies universally: every human power is ultimately an instrument of divine purpose.

verses.flow: In that day the remnant of Israel and the survivors of the house of Jacob will no more lean on him who struck them, but will lean on the LORD, the Holy One of Israel, in truth.
verses.note: MESSIANIC: "The remnant of Israel... will lean on the LORD, the Holy One of Israel, in truth." The remnant theme reaches its climax: those who survive judgment will finally place their trust where it belongs -- not on political alliances (Assyria, Egypt) but on God alone. Paul develops this remnant theology extensively in Romans 9-11.

verses.flow: A remnant will return, the remnant of Jacob, to the mighty God.
verses.note: MESSIANIC: "A remnant will return, the remnant of Jacob, to the mighty God." The "mighty God" ('El Gibbor') here is the same title given to the messianic child in 9:6 -- creating a deliberate theological link. The remnant returns to the Mighty God who is the child born to us.

words.g: the a sea or large body of water

verses.flow: For though your people Israel be as the sand of the sea, only a remnant of them will return. Destruction is decreed, overflowing with righteousness.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Though your people Israel be as the sand of the sea, only a remnant of them will return." Paul quotes this in Rom 9:27 as evidence that national Israel's unbelief does not void God's promises -- God always intended to save through a faithful remnant, not the nation as a whole. The promise to Abraham (Gen 22:17) of sand-like multitude is affirmed, but only a 'she'ar' returns.

verses.flow: For the Lord GOD of hosts will make a full end, as decreed, in the midst of all the earth.

verses.flow: And in that day his burden will depart from your shoulder, and his yoke from your neck; and the yoke will be broken because of the anointing."
verses.note: HEBREW: "The yoke will be broken because of the anointing" -- some translate 'mipne-shamen' as "because of fatness" (prosperity) but others connect 'shemen' to anointing (mashach/Messiah). The wordplay may be intentional: the yoke of oppression is ultimately broken by the Anointed One.

verses.flow: This very day he will halt at Nob; he will shake his fist at the mount of the daughter of Zion, the hill of Jerusalem.
Behold, the Lord, the LORD of hosts, will lop the boughs with terrifying power; and the great in height will be hewn down, and the lofty will be brought low.

verses.flow: And He will cut down the thickets of the forest with an axe, and Lebanon will fall by a majestic one.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Lebanon will fall by a majestic one" ('ba-addir') -- the proud forest of Lebanon represents Assyria in its might. The "majestic one" is God Himself. The chapter ends with the Assyrian forest hewn down, preparing for 11:1 where a shoot rises from Jesse's stump. The juxtaposition is deliberate: Assyria's great trees fall; from Israel's dead stump, the Messiah grows.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: And a shoot shall come forth from the stump of Jesse, and a branch from his roots shall bear fruit.
verses.note: MESSIANIC: "A shoot from the stump of Jesse" ('choter mi-geza Yishai') -- not from the stump of David, but of Jesse. The royal line will be cut down to its pre-royal origin; the dynasty appears dead. But from this dead stump comes new growth. The 'netser' (branch) is likely the root of "Nazarene" -- Matt 2:23 says Jesus was called a Nazarene to fulfill the prophets, possibly a wordplay on 'netser'. The movement from stump to shoot to fruit describes Messiah's emergence from apparent nothingness to full abundance.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd fem sing

verses.flow: And the Spirit of the LORD shall rest upon him, the spirit of wisdom and understanding, the spirit of counsel and might, the spirit of knowledge and the fear of the LORD.
verses.note: MESSIANIC: The sevenfold Spirit rests upon Messiah: (1) the Spirit of the LORD, (2) wisdom, (3) understanding, (4) counsel, (5) might, (6) knowledge, (7) the fear of the LORD. These correspond to the seven spirits before God's throne in Rev 1:4, 3:1, 4:5, 5:6. Luke 4:18 records Jesus reading Isa 61:1 ("The Spirit of the Lord is upon Me") and declaring "Today this Scripture is fulfilled in your hearing." The Spirit's "resting" ('nuach') implies permanent endowment, not temporary empowerment as with OT judges and kings.

words.m: conjunction + verb hiphil infinitive construct + suffix

verses.flow: but with righteousness he shall judge the poor, and decide with equity for the meek of the earth; and he shall strike the earth with the rod of his mouth, and with the breath of his lips he shall put the wicked to death.
verses.note: MESSIANIC: "He shall strike the earth with the rod of his mouth" -- the Messiah's weapon is His word. Rev 19:15 depicts Christ at His return: "From His mouth comes a sharp sword with which to strike down the nations." The Messiah judges not by appearance but by righteousness -- He sees the truth of every case. His justice especially favors the poor and meek ('aniyyim', 'anvei-erets').

verses.flow: And the wolf shall dwell with the lamb, and the leopard shall lie down with the young goat, and the calf and the lion and the fattened calf together; and a little child shall lead them.
verses.note: MESSIANIC: The wolf-lamb imagery describes the complete transformation of the natural order in the messianic kingdom. The curse of Gen 3 is reversed: predation ceases, hostility ends. "A little child shall lead them" -- the vulnerability of a child leading dangerous animals signals total peace. Romans 8:19-22 describes all creation groaning, awaiting this liberation.

verses.flow: They shall not hurt or destroy in all My holy mountain; for the earth shall be full of the knowledge of the LORD as the waters cover the sea.
verses.note: MESSIANIC: "The earth shall be full of the knowledge of the LORD as the waters cover the sea." This is the ultimate vision of the messianic age: universal knowledge of God, not merely information about God but relational, experiential knowing. Habakkuk 2:14 quotes this almost verbatim. Jer 31:34 promises: "They shall all know Me, from the least to the greatest." The "holy mountain" is Zion, the seat of Messiah's reign.

verses.flow: And in that day the root of Jesse, who shall stand as a signal for the peoples -- to him shall the nations seek, and his resting place shall be glorious.
verses.note: MESSIANIC: "The root of Jesse" ('shoresh Yishai') is quoted by Paul in Rom 15:12 as a prophecy of Christ. Note the paradox: in v.1 Messiah is a shoot FROM Jesse (descendant); in v.10 He is the ROOT OF Jesse (ancestor/source). He is both David's son and David's Lord (Matt 22:42-45). Rev 5:5 and 22:16 use the same title: "the Root and the Offspring of David." "His resting place shall be glorious" ('menuachato kavod') -- the Messiah's dwelling will be glory itself.

verses.flow: And it shall come to pass in that day that the Lord will again extend His hand a second time to recover the remnant of His people who remain, from Assyria, from Egypt, from Pathros, from Cush, from Elam, from Shinar, from Hamath, and from the coastlands of the sea.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "A second time" ('shenit') -- the first time was the Exodus from Egypt; this second recovery is the final, eschatological ingathering. The geographic spread is remarkable: Assyria, Egypt, Pathros (Upper Egypt), Cush (Ethiopia), Elam (Persia), Shinar (Babylon), Hamath (Syria), coastlands -- virtually the entire known world. God's people will be gathered from every direction.

verses.flow: And you will say in that day: "I will give thanks to You, O LORD, for though You were angry with me, Your anger turned away, that You might comfort me.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Though You were angry with me, Your anger turned away" -- this is the personal testimony of the redeemed remnant. God's anger is real but temporary; His comfort is the final word. The shift from "in that day" (eschatological) to "I will give thanks" (personal) shows that messianic salvation is experienced individually, not just nationally.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: Behold, God is my salvation; I will trust, and will not be afraid; for the LORD GOD is my strength and my song, and He has become my salvation."
verses.note: HEBREW: "The LORD GOD is my strength and my song" -- this exact phrase appears in Exod 15:2 (the Song of the Sea) and Ps 118:14. The Hebrew 'Yah YHWH' uses the shortened divine name, creating an intensified form. "He has become my salvation" ('vayehi li lishu'ah') -- 'yeshu'ah' is the noun form of Jesus' name (Yeshua). Isaiah's name (Yesha'yahu) means "the LORD saves"; Jesus' name means "the LORD is salvation." The entire chapter is a messianic hymn celebrating the salvation only God provides.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 2nd masc plur

verses.flow: With joy you will draw water from the wells of salvation.
verses.note: CHRIST TYPOLOGY: "Wells of salvation" ('ma'ayanei ha-yeshu'ah') -- Jesus uses water imagery extensively: "If anyone thirsts, let him come to Me and drink" (John 7:37); the woman at the well (John 4:14). Water drawn with joy from salvation's wells perfectly describes the believer's experience of Christ.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 2nd masc plur

verses.flow: And you will say in that day: "Give thanks to the LORD, call upon His name, make known His deeds among the peoples, proclaim that His name is exalted.
Sing praises to the LORD, for He has done gloriously; let this be made known in all the earth.

verses.flow: Shout and sing for joy, O inhabitant of Zion, for great in your midst is the Holy One of Israel."
verses.note: HEBREW: "The Holy One of Israel" ('qedosh Yisrael') closes this section as it opened (1:4). Chapters 1-12 form a literary unit: from indictment (ch.1) through judgment and messianic promise to songs of praise (ch.12). The Holy One who was "despised" in 1:4 is now "great in your midst" -- His holiness is no longer a threat but a cause for joy.

verses.flow: The oracle concerning Babylon which Isaiah the son of Amoz saw.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The "oracle" (Hebrew: 'massa') literally means "burden" -- a heavy word from God. The oracles against nations (chs.13-23) demonstrate that the LORD is not merely Israel's tribal deity but sovereign over all nations. He holds every empire accountable.

verses.flow: I Myself have commanded My consecrated ones, and have summoned My mighty warriors to execute My anger, My proudly exulting ones.
verses.note: HEBREW: "My consecrated ones" ('mequddashai') -- God's "holy ones" here are the Medes and Persians, pagan warriors whom God has set apart for His purpose without their knowledge. This parallels 10:5-7 where Assyria is God's instrument. Cyrus will later be called God's "anointed" ('mashiach', 45:1) -- the sovereign God uses whom He wills.

verses.note: HEBREW: "The day of the LORD" ('yom YHWH') is a major prophetic concept appearing in Joel, Amos, Zephaniah, and Malachi. "As destruction from the Almighty" -- the Hebrew creates a wordplay: 'ke-shod mi-Shaddai' (like devastation from the Devastator). The name Shaddai itself may derive from a root meaning "to overpower."

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Sheol beneath is stirred up to meet you" -- the underworld is personified, welcoming the fallen tyrant with bitter irony. The 'repha'im' (shades, departed spirits) rise from their thrones. This is one of the most vivid afterlife passages in the OT, depicting continued conscious existence after death. verses.flow: Your pomp is brought down to Sheol, the sound of your harps; maggots are the bed beneath you, and worms are your covering.
How you have fallen from heaven, O shining one, son of the dawn! How you are cut down to the ground, you who laid the nations low!
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "How you have fallen from heaven, O shining one, son of the dawn!" The Hebrew 'helel ben shachar' means "shining one, son of the dawn." The Latin Vulgate rendered 'helel' as "Lucifer" (light-bearer), which became a proper name in Christian tradition. The scholarly debate: the immediate context addresses the king of Babylon (v.4), using the mythological imagery of a divine being cast from heaven's assembly. The "five I will" statements (vv.13-14) describe the ultimate pride: self-deification. Jesus says "I saw Satan fall like lightning from heaven" (Luke 10:18), possibly alluding to this passage. Whether Isaiah describes a cosmic being (Satan), a human king, or both through prophetic telescoping remains debated. The divine council background is clear: the "mount of assembly in the far reaches of the north" ('har mo'ed be-yarketei tsafon') is God's throne mountain, and the aspiration to sit there is the supreme act of rebellion.

verses.flow: You said in your heart, 'I will ascend to heaven; above the stars of God I will set my throne on high; I will sit on the mount of assembly in the far reaches of the north;
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The five "I will" declarations represent the anatomy of pride: (1) "I will ascend to heaven" -- usurping God's realm; (2) "I will set my throne above the stars of God" -- ruling over angelic beings; (3) "I will sit on the mount of assembly" -- claiming God's throne; (4) "I will ascend above the heights of the clouds" -- surpassing divine glory; (5) "I will make myself like the Most High" -- total self-deification. The contrast with Christ in Phil 2:6-8 is absolute: where this figure grasps for equality with God, Christ "emptied Himself."

verses.note: MESSIANIC: "An altar to the LORD in the midst of Egypt" -- this is one of the most remarkable prophecies in Isaiah: Egypt, Israel's ancient oppressor, will worship the LORD. The altar and pillar ('matstsevah') are covenant markers. This anticipates the inclusion of Gentile nations in God's people, fulfilled in the church's expansion into Egypt (the Coptic church is one of the oldest in Christendom).

verses.flow: In that day there will be a highway from Egypt to Assyria, and Assyria will come into Egypt, and Egypt into Assyria, and the Egyptians will worship with the Assyrians.
verses.note: MESSIANIC: "A highway from Egypt to Assyria" -- the three great powers of the ancient world (Egypt, Israel, Assyria) are united in worship. This is eschatological universalism: enemies reconciled through shared worship of the LORD. The highway theme recurs in Isaiah (11:16; 35:8; 40:3) as a symbol of God's removing every barrier to His people's return.

verses.note: MESSIANIC: "Blessed be Egypt My people, and Assyria the work of My hands, and Israel My inheritance." This is breathtaking -- the titles previously reserved exclusively for Israel ("My people," "the work of My hands") are extended to Egypt and Assyria. This is the OT's clearest vision of Gentile inclusion in covenant relationship with God, anticipating Eph 2:11-22 where the dividing wall of hostility is destroyed in Christ.

verses.flow: at that time the LORD spoke by Isaiah the son of Amoz, saying, "Go, and loose the sackcloth from your waist and take off your sandals from your feet," and he did so, walking naked and barefoot.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Isaiah walks naked and barefoot for three years as a prophetic sign-act. This is among the most extreme enacted prophecies in Scripture. The Hebrew prophets embodied their messages: Hosea married a prostitute (Hos 1-3), Jeremiah wore a yoke (Jer 27-28), Ezekiel lay on his side for 390 days (Ezek 4). The prophet's body becomes the sermon. Isaiah's nakedness foreshadows Egypt and Cush's humiliation (v.4).

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "We, how shall we escape?" -- the inhabitants of the coastland (likely Philistia or Judah) realize the folly of trusting Egypt and Cush against Assyria. If these mighty nations fell, what hope has anyone who relied on them? This is the political lesson Isaiah drives relentlessly: trust in human alliances leads to catastrophe; only trust in God endures.

verses.flow: And behold, here come riders, horsemen in pairs!" And he answered and said, "Fallen, fallen is Babylon; and all the carved images of her gods he has shattered to the ground."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Fallen, fallen is Babylon" -- the doubled declaration of Babylon's fall is echoed verbatim in Rev 14:8 and 18:2. John's Apocalypse deliberately draws on Isaiah's oracle, extending historical Babylon's fall to the eschatological judgment of the entire world system that opposes God. The shattering of carved images on the ground reverses the pride of idolatry.

verses.flow: O my threshed and winnowed one, O son of my threshing floor! What I have heard from the LORD of hosts, the God of Israel, I announce to you.
The oracle concerning Dumah. One is calling to me from Seir, "Watchman, what time of the night? Watchman, what time of the night?"
verses.note: HEBREW: "Watchman, what time of the night?" ('shomer mah mi-llailah') -- the oracle of Dumah (Edom) is the shortest and most enigmatic in Isaiah. "Dumah" means "silence" -- possibly a wordplay on Edom. The question about the night's remaining hours is a plea for hope: when will the oppression end? The watchman's answer is ambiguous: "Morning comes, and also the night" -- dawn and darkness both lie ahead. The invitation to "come back again" leaves the question open, suggesting that Edom's fate depends on its response to God.

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Let us eat and drink, for tomorrow we die" -- Paul quotes this in 1 Cor 15:32 as the logical conclusion of life without resurrection. If there is no judgment and no afterlife, hedonism is rational. Isaiah condemns it as the response of a people who have abandoned faith in God's purposes.

verses.note: HEBREW: "Shebna, who is over the household" -- 'asher al ha-bayit' is the title of the chief steward, the highest non-royal official in the kingdom. Shebna is the only individual in Isaiah's oracles against nations to receive a personal oracle of judgment. His sin: self-aggrandizement -- cutting an elaborate tomb for himself (v.16) while the nation faces existential crisis. He is replaced by Eliakim (v.20), who becomes a type of Christ.

verses.note: MESSIANIC: "I will place on his shoulder the key of the house of David. He shall open, and none shall shut; and he shall shut, and none shall open." Jesus applies this directly to Himself in Rev 3:7: "The words of the holy one, the true one, who has the key of David, who opens and no one will shut, who shuts and no one opens." Eliakim is a type of Christ: given authority over the house, the key on his shoulder (cf. "the government will rest on His shoulders," 9:6), and the power of absolute access. The "key" ('mafteach') represents plenary authority over who enters and who is excluded from the king's presence.

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Before Me there was no God formed, and there will be none after Me" — the strongest monotheistic declaration in the OT. YHWH is not the highest god among gods; He is the only God, period. The "witnesses" theme makes Israel responsible to testify to this truth.

verses.flow: It is I who says of Cyrus, 'He is My shepherd! And he will perform all My desire.' And he declares of Jerusalem, 'She will be built,' and of the temple, 'Your foundation will be laid.'"
verses.note: MESSIANIC: Cyrus named by name ~150 years before his birth — one of the most striking prophetic predictions in Scripture. Called "My shepherd" — a title normally reserved for Davidic kings and God Himself. God uses a pagan ruler to accomplish His redemptive purposes. THEOLOGICAL: The temple foundation decree is historically confirmed by the Cyrus Cylinder (539 BC).

verses.flow: Thus says the LORD to His anointed, to Cyrus, whose right hand I have grasped, to subdue nations before him, and to loose the loins of kings, to open doors before him so that gates will not be shut:
verses.note: HEBREW: mashiach (anointed one) -- Cyrus is the only Gentile in the entire Hebrew Bible to receive this title. The word mashiach is the same word used for Israel's kings and priests, and is the root of "Messiah." God calls a pagan Persian emperor "His anointed" to accomplish His purposes -- shattering Israel's assumption that God works only through Israel. Cyrus conquered Babylon in 539 BC and issued the decree allowing the Jews to return (Ezra 1:1-4). The prophecy is stunning: Isaiah wrote ca. 700 BC, naming Cyrus by name over 150 years before his birth.

verses.flow: I am the LORD, and there is no other; besides Me there is no God. I will gird you, though you have not known Me,
verses.note: HEBREW: "I am the LORD (YHWH), and there is no other (we'en od)" -- this declaration of absolute monotheism is hammered repeatedly in Isaiah 40-48 (see 44:6, 44:8, 45:5, 45:6, 45:14, 45:18, 45:21, 45:22, 46:9). It is the theological foundation for the universal salvation announced in 45:22.

verses.flow: that men may know from the rising to the setting of the sun that there is no one besides Me. I am the LORD, and there is no other,
the One forming light and creating darkness, making peace and creating calamity; I am the LORD who does all these things.
verses.note: HEBREW: "forming light and creating darkness, making peace (shalom) and creating calamity (ra)" -- God claims sovereignty over all reality, including what humans call evil. The word ra here means calamity/disaster, not moral evil. This verse was directed against Persian dualism (Zoroastrianism), which posited two gods: one of light, one of darkness. YHWH claims both domains.

verses.flow: Woe to the one who quarrels with his Maker — a potsherd among the potsherds of the earth! Will the clay say to the potter, "What are you making?" Or the thing you are making say, "He has no hands"?
verses.note: CHRIST: Paul quotes this potter/clay metaphor in Romans 9:20-21 to argue God's sovereign right to show mercy to whom He wills. The potsherd (cheresh) has no standing to interrogate the Potter. This is not a denial of human dignity but an affirmation of divine prerogative.

verses.flow: Turn to Me and be saved, all the ends of the earth; for I am God, and there is no other.
verses.note: CHRIST: "Turn to Me and be saved, all the ends of the earth" -- this is one of the most sweeping universalist salvation texts in the OT. God's offer extends to ALL humanity, not just Israel. Paul explicitly applies v.23 to Christ in Philippians 2:10-11: "at the name of Jesus every knee will bow... every tongue will confess." The Isaiah context is YHWH speaking -- Paul transfers YHWH's prerogative to Jesus, an extraordinary christological claim.

verses.flow: I have sworn by Myself, the word has gone forth from My mouth in righteousness and will not turn back, that to Me every knee will bow, every tongue will swear allegiance.
verses.note: HEBREW: "I have sworn by Myself" (bi nishba'ti) -- since there is none greater to swear by, God swears by His own being. The oath is irrevocable (cf. Heb 6:13-18). "Every knee will bow, every tongue will swear allegiance" -- this is not optional. It is eschatological certainty. In Phil 2:10-11 Paul applies this to Christ's universal lordship.

verses.flow: In the LORD all the offspring of Israel will be justified and will glory.
verses.note: HEBREW: "In the LORD all the offspring of Israel will be justified (yitsdaqu) and will glory (yithallelu)" -- the verb tsadaq (to be righteous/justified) is forensic: declared righteous. This is the OT root of Paul's doctrine of justification by faith (Rom 3:21-26). Righteousness comes "in the LORD," not from human merit.

verses.flow: Bel has bowed down, Nebo stoops over; their images are consigned to the beasts and the cattle. The things that you carry are burdensome, a load for the weary beast.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Bel (= Marduk, chief god of Babylon) and Nebo (= Nabu, god of writing and wisdom, patron of Nebuchadnezzar whose name means "Nebo, protect my boundary") -- these gods must be CARRIED by beasts of burden. They are dead weight. In contrast, YHWH carries His people (v.3-4). The irony is devastating: pagan gods are cargo; Israel's God is the porter.

verses.flow: They stooped over, they have bowed down together; they could not rescue the burden, but have themselves gone into captivity.
Listen to Me, O house of Jacob, and all the remnant of the house of Israel, you who have been borne by Me from birth and have been carried from the womb:

verses.flow: even to your old age I am He, and even to gray hairs I will carry you. I have made, and I will bear; I will carry and will deliver you.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Even to your old age I am He, and even to gray hairs I will carry you" -- one of the most tender promises in Isaiah. YHWH reverses the idol metaphor: you don't carry Me; I carry you. From womb to grave, God bears His people. The fourfold progression -- "I have made, I will bear, I will carry, I will deliver" -- covers all of life.

verses.flow: Remember this, and be assured; bring it back to mind, you transgressors.
Remember the former things long past, for I am God, and there is no other; I am God, and there is no one like Me,
declaring the end from the beginning, and from ancient times things which have not been done, saying, "My purpose will be established, and I will accomplish all My good pleasure";
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Declaring the end from the beginning" (maggid me-reshit acharit) -- God's predictive prophecy is proof of His deity. Unlike idols, He knows the future because He ordained it. This is the theological basis for biblical prophecy and the reason Isaiah can name Cyrus 150 years in advance.

verses.flow: Come down and sit in the dust, O virgin daughter of Babylon; sit on the ground without a throne, O daughter of the Chaldeans! For you shall no more be called tender and delicate.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The "virgin daughter of Babylon" -- Babylon had never been conquered when Isaiah prophesied. She was the "virgin" fortress of the ancient world. The title is ironic: she will be violated. This oracle is a taunt song (mashal) against the world empire that would destroy Jerusalem in 586 BC.

verses.flow: Yet you said, "I will be a queen forever." These things you did not consider nor remember the outcome of them.
Now, then, hear this, you sensual one, who dwells securely, who says in her heart, "I am, and there is no one besides me. I will not sit as a widow, nor will I know loss of children."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "I am, and there is no one besides me" (ani we'afsi od) -- Babylon claims for herself the exact language YHWH uses (45:5-6). This is the essence of idolatry: the creature claiming the prerogatives of the Creator. The same self-deification appears in the king of Babylon (14:13-14) and in the eschatological Babylon of Revelation 18:7.

verses.flow: But these two things will come on you suddenly in one day: loss of children and widowhood. They will come on you in full measure in spite of your many sorceries, in spite of the great power of your spells.
verses.note: PROPHETIC: "These two things will come on you suddenly in one day" -- Babylon fell in a single night (Dan 5:30-31, October 539 BC). The city that thought herself invulnerable was overthrown in hours. This pattern of sudden judgment recurs in Revelation 18:8: "in one day her plagues will come."

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "For My own sake, for My own sake, I will act" -- the ultimate ground of God's saving action is not human merit but divine self-glory. God acts for the sake of His name (cf. verses.flow: Come near to Me, listen to this: from the beginning I have not spoken in secret, from the time it took place, I was there. And now the Lord GOD has sent Me, and His Spirit.
verses.note: TRINITARIAN: "And now the Lord GOD has sent Me, and His Spirit" -- the Speaker (who has been identified as YHWH throughout ch.48) now says He has been sent by the Lord GOD and His Spirit. This is one of the most remarkable trinitarian hints in the OT: a divine figure sent by another divine figure and the Spirit. The early church fathers cited this text extensively.

verses.note: CHRIST: "The LORD has redeemed His servant Jacob" -- ga'al (redeemed) is kinsman-redeemer language. The same verb is used of Boaz in Ruth and of God throughout Isaiah 40-66. Redemption requires a kinsman -- one who is both related and able. This is the theological logic of the Incarnation.

verses.note: STRUCTURAL: "There is no peace for the wicked" -- this refrain divides Isaiah 40-66 into three sections: 40-48 (ending here), 49-57 (ending at 57:21), and 58-66. Each section ends with a warning that God's salvation does not extend to the impenitent.

verses.flow: Listen to Me, O islands, and pay attention, you peoples from afar. The LORD called Me from the womb; from the body of My mother He named Me.
verses.note: SECOND SERVANT SONG (49:1-7): The Servant speaks in first person, echoing Jeremiah's call (Jer 1:5). "The LORD called Me from the womb" -- prenatal calling indicates a unique, predestined mission. The Servant is distinguished from Israel (He has a mission TO Israel, v.5-6), yet is called "Israel" (v.3). This paradox resolves in Christ: He is the true Israel, the faithful remnant of One, who accomplishes what the nation could not.

verses.flow: He said to Me, "You are My Servant, Israel, in whom I will show My glory."
verses.note: HEBREW: "You are My Servant, Israel, in whom I will show My glory" -- the Servant is named "Israel" but is distinct from the nation (v.5 says His mission is to bring Jacob/Israel back). The resolution: the Servant is the ideal Israel, the One who fulfills Israel's calling. Christ is the true vine (John 15:1) where Israel was the failed vine (Isa 5).

verses.flow: But I said, "I have toiled in vain, I have spent My strength for nothing and vanity; yet surely My cause is with the LORD, and My reward with My God."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "I have toiled in vain" -- the Servant experiences apparent failure. His ministry to Israel seems fruitless. Yet He trusts that His cause is with the LORD. This anticipates Jesus' rejection by His own people (John 1:11) and His trust in the Father even at the cross.

verses.flow: He says, "It is too small a thing that You should be My Servant to raise up the tribes of Jacob and to restore the preserved ones of Israel; I will also make You a light of the nations so that My salvation may reach to the end of the earth."
verses.note: CHRIST: "I will also make You a light of the nations (or le-goyim) so that My salvation may reach to the end of the earth" -- this is THE pivotal verse for the Gentile mission. The Servant's task exceeds restoring Israel; He must bring salvation to the entire world. Simeon quotes this at Jesus' presentation (Luke 2:32). Paul and Barnabas quote it to justify the Gentile mission (Acts 13:47). The universal scope of the gospel is rooted here.

verses.flow: Can a woman forget her nursing child and have no compassion on the son of her womb? Even these may forget, but I will not forget you.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Can a woman forget her nursing child?" -- God uses the most primal human bond (nursing mother/infant) as an analogy, then transcends it: "Even these may forget, but I will not forget you." God's love exceeds the deepest human love. The Hebrew racham (compassion) is from rechem (womb) -- God's compassion is maternal, visceral.

words.m: verb qal perfect 1st common sing + suffix

verses.flow: Behold, I have inscribed you on the palms of My hands; your walls are continually before Me.
verses.note: CHRIST: "I have inscribed you on the palms of My hands" (al kappayim chaqotikh) -- chaqaq means to engrave, to cut into. Zion's name is permanently cut into God's palms. The early church saw crucifixion typology here: Christ's hands were pierced/inscribed on Calvary. Whether or not Isaiah intended this, the resonance is extraordinary -- the wounds in Jesus' hands are the permanent marks of His love for His people (cf. John 20:27).

verses.flow: Thus says the LORD: "Where is the certificate of divorce by which I have sent your mother away? Or to whom of My creditors did I sell you? Behold, you were sold for your iniquities, and for your transgressions your mother was sent away.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: God has not divorced Israel. There is no certificate of divorce (sepher keritut). The exile was discipline, not abandonment. The marriage metaphor (God as husband, Israel as wife) runs through the prophets (Hos 2, Jer 3, Ezek 16). God remains faithful even when His bride is unfaithful.

verses.flow: Why was there no man when I came? When I called, why was there none to answer? Is My hand so short that it cannot ransom? Or have I no power to deliver? Behold, I dry up the sea with My rebuke, I make the rivers a wilderness; their fish stink for lack of water and die of thirst.
I clothe the heavens with blackness and make sackcloth their covering."
The Lord GOD has given Me the tongue of disciples, that I may know how to sustain the weary one with a word. He awakens Me morning by morning, He awakens My ear to listen as a disciple.
verses.note: THIRD SERVANT SONG (50:4-9): The Servant has "the tongue of disciples" (leshon limmudim) -- He is both Teacher and Learner, perfectly obedient to the Father. "He awakens Me morning by morning" -- the Servant's daily communion with God is the source of His authority. This prefigures Jesus' pattern of early morning prayer (Mark 1:35) and His declaration "I do nothing on My own initiative" (John 8:28).

verses.flow: The Lord GOD has opened My ear; and I was not disobedient nor did I turn back.
I gave My back to those who strike Me, and My cheeks to those who pluck out the beard; I did not cover My face from humiliation and spitting.
verses.note: CHRIST: "I gave My back to those who strike Me, and My cheeks to those who pluck out the beard" -- this is graphic physical abuse: flogging (the back) and beard-plucking (an extreme insult in the ancient Near East). "I did not cover My face from humiliation and spitting" -- fulfilled precisely in Christ's passion (Matt 26:67, 27:26, 27:30; Mark 14:65, 15:19). The Servant endures all this voluntarily -- "I gave" (natati), not "they took."

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 1st common sing

verses.flow: For the Lord GOD helps Me, therefore I am not disgraced. Therefore I have set My face like flint, and I know that I will not be ashamed.
verses.note: HEBREW: "I have set My face like flint" (ka-challamish samti panay) -- challamish is the hardest stone, flint. The Servant's resolve is unbreakable. Luke uses the same imagery: "He set His face to go to Jerusalem" (Luke 9:51). Knowing what awaits Him, He does not turn back. This is holy determination, not stoic indifference.

verses.flow: He who vindicates Me is near; who will contend with Me? Let us stand up to each other; who has a case against Me? Let him draw near to Me.

verses.flow: Indeed, the LORD will comfort Zion; He will comfort all her waste places. And He will make her wilderness like Eden, and her desert like the garden of the LORD; joy and gladness will be found in her, thanksgiving and the sound of a melody.
verses.note: ESCHATOLOGICAL: "He will make her wilderness like Eden" -- the new creation reverses the curse of Genesis 3. The final state is not merely restoration but Edenic paradise. This theme culminates in Rev 22:1-3, where the tree of life reappears and the curse is removed.

verses.flow: Lift up your eyes to the sky, then look to the earth beneath; for the sky will vanish like smoke, and the earth will wear out like a garment, and its inhabitants will die in like manner; but My salvation will be forever, and My righteousness will not be broken.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "The sky will vanish like smoke, and the earth will wear out like a garment" -- the created order is temporary; God's righteousness is eternal. Jesus echoes this: "Heaven and earth will pass away, but My words will not pass away" (Matt 24:35). Peter applies it: "the heavens will be destroyed by burning" (2 Pet 3:10-12).

verses.flow: How beautiful on the mountains are the feet of him who brings good news, who announces peace, who brings good news of happiness, who announces salvation, and says to Zion, "Your God reigns!"
verses.note: CHRIST: "How beautiful on the mountains are the feet of him who brings good news" (mevasser) -- the mevasser is the herald who announces victory. Paul quotes this in Romans 10:15 for the preaching of the gospel. The "good news" (besorah) is the OT root of the Greek euangelion (gospel). The content of the good news is: "Your God reigns!" -- the kingdom of God has come. This is what Jesus proclaimed: "The kingdom of God is at hand" (Mark 1:15).

verses.flow: Behold, My Servant will prosper, He will be high and lifted up and greatly exalted.
verses.note: FOURTH SERVANT SONG BEGINS (52:13-53:12): This is the most important messianic prophecy in the Hebrew Bible. The song has five stanzas (52:13-15; 53:1-3; 53:4-6; 53:7-9; 53:10-12), each with three verses. YHWH speaks in 52:13-15 and 53:11b-12; the people speak in 53:1-10; then YHWH concludes. "My Servant will prosper (yaskil), He will be high (yarum) and lifted up (wenissa) and greatly exalted (wegavah me'od)" -- three words for exaltation, the same language used of YHWH Himself in 6:1 ("high and lifted up"). The Servant shares YHWH's own throne-language.

verses.flow: Just as many were astonished at you — His appearance was marred more than any man and His form more than the sons of men —
verses.note: CHRIST: "His appearance was marred more than any man (mishchat me'ish mar'ehu) and His form more than the sons of men" -- mishchat means disfigured, ruined beyond recognition. This is the scourging of Christ, the beating that left Him unrecognizable (Matt 27:26-31). The Roman flagellum embedded with bone and metal tore flesh to the bone. The contrast with v.13 (exaltation) and v.15 (nations/kings silenced) creates the great paradox: supreme glory through supreme suffering.

verses.flow: so He will sprinkle many nations; kings will shut their mouths on account of Him; for what had not been told them they will see, and what they had not heard they will understand.
verses.note: HEBREW: "He will sprinkle (yazzeh) many nations" -- the verb nazzah means to sprinkle, the priestly act of sprinkling sacrificial blood for purification (Lev 4:6, 16:14-19). The Servant is both sacrifice AND priest, sprinkling His blood on nations (not just Israel). Kings will shut their mouths in stunned silence at what they see -- the crucified One revealed as the Lord of all.

verses.flow: Who has believed our report? And to whom has the arm of the LORD been revealed?
verses.note: CHRIST: "Who has believed our report?" (mi he'emin lishmu'atenu) -- John quotes this verse to explain why Israel rejected Jesus despite His miracles (John 12:38). Paul quotes it for faith coming by hearing (Rom 10:16). The "arm of the LORD" is the Servant Himself -- God's power revealed not in military might but in a suffering, dying man. This is the great scandal: God's arm looks like weakness.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing

conjunction + verb qal imperfect 1st common plur + suffix

verses.flow: For He grew up before Him like a tender shoot, and like a root out of parched ground; He has no stately form or majesty that we should look upon Him, nor appearance that we should be attracted to Him.
verses.note: CHRIST: "A tender shoot" (yoneq) and "a root out of parched ground" (shoresh me'erets tsiyyah) -- the Messiah's origin is humble, not royal. No stately form (to'ar), no majesty (hadar). He comes from Nazareth, a nothing town (John 1:46). He is a carpenter's son (Matt 13:55). The world expected a conquering king; God sent a servant from the dust.

words.m: verb qal perfect 1st common plur + suffix

verses.flow: He was despised and forsaken of men, a man of sorrows and acquainted with grief; and like one from whom men hide their face He was despised, and we did not esteem Him.
verses.note: HEBREW: "A man of sorrows (ish makhovot) and acquainted with grief (yadu'a choli)" -- makhovot means pains, both physical and emotional. choli means sickness, suffering. The Servant is not occasionally sad but DEFINED by suffering. "Like one from whom men hide their face" -- He provokes revulsion. This is the crucified Christ: bloody, disfigured, repulsive to the watching crowd. "We did not esteem Him" -- Israel's verdict, reversed only by the resurrection.

words.m: verb qal perfect 3rd masc sing + suffix
verb qal perfect 1st common plur + suffix

verses.flow: Surely our griefs He Himself bore, and our sorrows He carried; yet we ourselves esteemed Him stricken, smitten of God, and afflicted.
verses.note: CHRIST: "Surely our griefs He Himself bore (nasa), and our sorrows He carried (sevalm)" -- nasa means to lift up, bear, carry away. The Servant does not merely sympathize; He substitutes. He takes our suffering upon Himself. Matthew 8:17 applies this to Jesus' healing ministry, but the full meaning is the cross. "We esteemed Him stricken (nagu'a), smitten (mukkeh) of God" -- the onlookers thought God was punishing HIM for His own sins. They were wrong. The punishment was vicarious.

verses.flow: But He was pierced for our transgressions, He was crushed for our iniquities; the chastening for our peace fell upon Him, and by His wounds we are healed.
verses.note: CHRIST: THE HEART OF THE ATONEMENT. "He was pierced (mecholal) for our transgressions (pesha'enu)" -- chalal means pierced, wounded fatally. pesha is willful rebellion. "He was crushed (medukka) for our iniquities (avonoteinu)" -- dakka means pulverized. "The chastening (musar) for our peace (shelomenu) fell upon Him" -- musar is disciplinary punishment. The punishment that produces OUR shalom (wholeness, peace with God) was inflicted on HIM. "By His wounds (chaburato) we are healed (nirpa)" -- chaburah means stripe, welt from a blow. The grammar is emphatic: HIS wounds, OUR healing. This is penal substitutionary atonement in its purest OT form. 1 Peter 2:24 quotes this directly.

words.g: as/like a collective name for a flock

verses.flow: All of us like sheep have gone astray, each of us has turned to his own way; but the LORD has caused the iniquity of us all to fall on Him.
verses.note: CHRIST: "All of us like sheep have gone astray" (kullanu ka-tson ta'inu) -- universal human guilt. "Each of us has turned to his own way" (ish le-darko paninu) -- sin is self-direction, autonomy from God. "The LORD has caused the iniquity of us all to fall on Him" (YHWH hipgi'a bo et avon kullanu) -- hipgi'a means to cause to strike, to lay upon. God the Father is the active agent who transfers human guilt onto the Servant. This is not an accident; it is divine design. The sin of ALL falls on ONE.

words.g: as/like a member of a flock

verses.flow: He was oppressed and He was afflicted, yet He did not open His mouth; like a lamb that is led to slaughter, and like a sheep that is silent before its shearers, so He did not open His mouth.
verses.note: CHRIST: "Like a lamb that is led to slaughter" (ka-seh la-tevach yuval) -- seh is the general term for a sheep/lamb, the same word used for the Passover lamb (Exod 12:3) and Abraham's substitute offering (Gen 22:8 -- "God will provide for Himself the lamb"). The Servant's silence before His accusers is fulfilled in Christ's trial (Matt 26:63, 27:12-14; Mark 15:5; 1 Pet 2:23). Philip explains this passage to the Ethiopian eunuch (Acts 8:32-35) as referring to Jesus.

verses.flow: By oppression and judgment He was taken away; and as for His generation, who considered that He was cut off out of the land of the living for the transgression of my people, to whom the stroke was due?
verses.note: HEBREW: "He was cut off out of the land of the living" (nigzar me'erets chayyim) -- gazar means to cut, sever. The Servant dies. "For the transgression of my people (ammi), to whom the stroke was due" -- the punishment that belonged to "my people" fell on Him. This is vicarious, substitutionary death: He dies in the place of others. The "my" is either Isaiah or YHWH speaking.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: His grave was assigned with wicked men, yet He was with a rich man in His death, because He had done no violence, nor was there any deceit in His mouth.
verses.note: CHRIST: "His grave was assigned with wicked men (resha'im), yet He was with a rich man (ashir) in His death (bemotav)" -- this is fulfilled with astonishing precision. Jesus was crucified between two criminals (wicked men, Matt 27:38) but buried in the tomb of Joseph of Arimathea, a rich man (Matt 27:57-60). The dual detail -- wicked in death, rich in burial -- is too specific to be coincidence. "He had done no violence, nor was there any deceit in His mouth" -- the Servant's innocence is emphasized. He did not deserve any of it.

verses.flow: But the LORD was pleased to crush Him, putting Him to grief; if He would render Himself as a guilt offering, He will see His offspring, He will prolong His days, and the good pleasure of the LORD will prosper in His hand.
verses.note: CHRIST: "The LORD was pleased (chaphets) to crush Him" -- chaphets means to delight in, to take pleasure in. This is the most theologically staggering statement in the chapter: the Father WILLED the Son's suffering. It was not tragedy but divine plan. "If He would render Himself as a guilt offering (asham)" -- asham is the technical term for the guilt/trespass offering of Leviticus 5-7, the sacrifice that atones for specific sins and requires restitution. The Servant IS the sacrifice. "He will see His offspring (zera)" -- a dead man cannot see offspring. This implies RESURRECTION. The Servant dies (v.8-9), becomes a guilt offering (v.10a), then sees offspring and prolongs His days (v.10b). Death is not the end. This is the earliest and clearest OT prophecy of resurrection following atoning death.

verses.flow: As a result of the anguish of His soul, He will see it and be satisfied; by His knowledge the Righteous One, My Servant, will justify the many, for He will bear their iniquities.
verses.note: CHRIST: "The Righteous One (tsaddiq), My Servant, will justify (yatsdiq) the many (la-rabbim)" -- the wordplay is deliberate: the Righteous One makes others righteous. This is imputed righteousness: His righteousness credited to others. "By His knowledge" (beda'to) -- through knowing Him, or through what He knows/experiences. Paul develops this fully in Romans 5:18-19: "through one act of righteousness there resulted justification of life to all men." The "many" (rabbim) is not "some" but "the great number" -- a Hebraism for multitude.

words.m: conjunction + preposition + verb qal participle active

verses.flow: Therefore, I will allot Him a portion with the great, and He will divide the spoil with the strong; because He poured out Himself to death, and was numbered with the transgressors; yet He Himself bore the sin of many, and interceded for the transgressors.
verses.note: CHRIST: "He poured out Himself (he'erah) to death" -- he'erah means to empty, pour out (cf. Phil 2:7, kenosis). "He was numbered with the transgressors (posh'im)" -- fulfilled at the crucifixion between two criminals (Mark 15:28, Luke 22:37). "He Himself bore the sin of many" -- repeating the substitutionary theme of v.4-6. "And interceded (yaphgi'a) for the transgressors" -- the Servant prays for the very people who kill Him. Jesus on the cross: "Father, forgive them, for they do not know what they are doing" (Luke 23:34). The song ends with the Servant alive and active (interceding), confirming the resurrection implied in v.10.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal imperative 2nd fem sing

verses.flow: "Shout for joy, O barren one, you who have borne no child; break forth into joyful shouting and cry aloud, you who have not travailed; for the sons of the desolate one will be more numerous than the sons of the married woman," says the LORD.
verses.note: CHRIST: Paul quotes this verse in Galatians 4:27 to argue that the "barren" woman (the heavenly Jerusalem, the church of faith) has more children than the "married" woman (the earthly Jerusalem, the law). The barren one's joy is eschatological: her children are born by promise, not by flesh.

verses.flow: In righteousness you will be established; you will be far from oppression, for you will not fear; and from terror, for it will not come near you.

Behold, I Myself have created the smith who blows the fire of coals and brings out a weapon for its work; and I have created the destroyer to ruin.

No weapon that is formed against you will prosper; and every tongue that accuses you in judgment you will condemn. This is the heritage of the servants of the LORD, and their vindication is from Me," declares the LORD.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "No weapon that is formed against you will prosper" -- one of the great assurance texts. The promise is not that no weapon will be formed (attacks will come) but that none will succeed. This is corporate promise to God's people, not individual prosperity.

The invitation is universal ("every one"), unconditional ("without money"), and abundant ("wine and milk"). This is grace: you cannot buy it, you cannot earn it, you can only receive it.

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "My thoughts are not your thoughts, nor are your ways My ways" -- often quoted out of context as mere divine mystery. In context, it explains why God ABUNDANTLY PARDONS (v.7). Humans hold grudges; God forgives lavishly. The gap between divine and human is not primarily intellectual but moral: God's mercy exceeds anything humans can imagine.

verses.flow: The Spirit of the Lord GOD is upon me, because the LORD has anointed me to bring good news to the afflicted; He has sent me to bind up the brokenhearted, to proclaim liberty to captives and freedom to prisoners;
verses.note: CHRIST: "The Spirit of the Lord GOD is upon me, because the LORD has anointed me" -- Jesus reads this text in the synagogue at Nazareth (Luke 4:18-19) and declares: "Today this Scripture has been fulfilled in your hearing" (Luke 4:21). This is the most explicit self-identification Jesus makes with an OT text. He claims to BE the anointed Servant of Isaiah. Critically, Jesus STOPS READING in the middle of v.2, after "the favorable year of the LORD" -- He does NOT read "the day of vengeance of our God." The favorable year is the first coming (grace); the day of vengeance is the second coming (judgment). The gap between the two phrases is the entire church age.

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "The favorable year of the LORD" (shenat ratson la-YHWH) -- ratson means acceptance, favor, good will. This echoes the Year of Jubilee (Lev 25), when debts were cancelled, slaves freed, and land returned. Jesus' ministry is the ultimate Jubilee: cancellation of the debt of sin, freedom for captives of Satan, restoration of the inheritance lost in Adam.

verses.flow: And they will call them, "The holy people, the redeemed of the LORD"; and you will be called, "Sought out, a city not forsaken."

Who is this who comes from Edom, with garments of glowing colors from Bozrah, this One who is majestic in His apparel, marching in the greatness of His strength? "It is I who speak in righteousness, mighty to save."
verses.note: ESCHATOLOGICAL: "Who is this who comes from Edom, with garments of glowing colors from Bozrah?" -- the divine warrior returns from battle with blood-stained garments. Edom (= Esau's territory) represents the enemies of God's people. Revelation 19:13-15 directly echoes this: "He is clothed with a robe dipped in blood... He treads the wine press of the fierce wrath of God." The stained garments are not the warrior's own blood but the blood of His enemies. This is Christ at the Second Coming -- not the Lamb led to slaughter (53:7) but the Lion of Judah executing judgment.

verses.flow: For all of us have become like one who is unclean, and all our righteous deeds are like a filthy garment; and all of us wither like a leaf, and our iniquities, like the wind, take us away.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "All our righteous deeds are like a filthy garment" (ke-veged iddim) -- iddim refers to menstrual cloths, the most ritually unclean object in Israelite culture. Even our BEST acts are contaminated by sin. This is not nihilism but honesty: human righteousness cannot save. Only God's righteousness (the gift of 61:10) can clothe us.

verses.flow: There is no one who calls on Your name, who arouses himself to take hold of You; for You have hidden Your face from us and have delivered us into the power of our iniquities.
But now, O LORD, You are our Father; we are the clay, and You our potter; and all of us are the work of Your hand.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "We are the clay, and You our potter" -- echoing 45:9 and Jeremiah 18:1-6. The potter has sovereign authority over the clay. Yet the clay appeals: "do not be angry beyond measure" (v.9). Sovereignty and prayer are not contradictory; they coexist in the biblical worldview. God is sovereign AND responsive to His people's cries.

verses.flow: Because he who is blessed in the earth will be blessed by the God of truth; and he who swears in the earth will swear by the God of truth; because the former troubles are forgotten, and because they are hidden from My sight!
For behold, I create new heavens and a new earth; and the former things will not be remembered or come to mind.
verses.note: ESCHATOLOGICAL: "Behold, I create new heavens and a new earth" (bore shamayim chadashim wa'arets chadashah) -- the verb bara (create) is the same used in Genesis 1:1. This is not renovation but NEW CREATION. The former things "will not be remembered or come to mind" -- the new creation so surpasses the old that the old is forgotten. 2 Peter 3:13 and Revelation 21:1 both quote this directly. This is the ultimate hope of biblical faith: not escape from the material world but its total renewal.

verses.flow: No longer will there be in it an infant who lives but a few days, or an old man who does not live out his days; for the youth will die at the age of one hundred, and the one who does not reach the age of one hundred will be thought accursed.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The description of the new creation includes longevity but not the abolition of death -- "the youth will die at the age of one hundred." This is puzzling if read as the final eternal state (where Rev 21:4 says "there will no longer be any death"). Many scholars see this as an intermediate messianic kingdom (the millennium of Rev 20), with the fully deathless state in 66:22-24 and Rev 21-22.

They will not build and another inhabit, they will not plant and another eat; for as the lifetime of a tree, so will be the days of My people, and My chosen ones will wear out the work of their hands.
They will not labor in vain, or bear children for calamity; for they are the offspring of those blessed by the LORD, and their descendants with them.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: It will also come to pass that before they call, I will answer; and while they are still speaking, I will hear.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Before they call, I will answer" -- in the new creation, the distance between God and His people is eliminated. Prayer is answered before it is uttered. This is the intimacy Adam had in Eden, restored and surpassed.

words.g: a mountain or range of hills

verses.flow: The wolf and the lamb will graze together, and the lion will eat straw like the ox; and dust will be the serpent's food. They will do no evil or harm in all My holy mountain," says the LORD.
verses.note: ESCHATOLOGICAL: "The wolf and the lamb will graze together" -- echoing 11:6-9, the Edenic peace vision. The curse on the serpent remains ("dust will be the serpent's food"), but the rest of creation is harmonized. The new creation reverses the fall but retains the judgment on the serpent (Gen 3:14).

verses.flow: Thus says the LORD: "Heaven is My throne and the earth is My footstool. Where then is a house you could build for Me? And where is a place that I may rest?
verses.note: CHRIST: Stephen quotes this in Acts 7:49-50 in his speech before the Sanhedrin, arguing that God does not dwell in temples made with hands. "Heaven is My throne and the earth is My footstool" -- God cannot be contained by any building. The temple pointed to a greater reality: God's dwelling with His people (Rev 21:3).

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc plur

verses.flow: For My hand made all these things, thus all these things came into being," declares the LORD. "But to this one I will look, to him who is humble and contrite of spirit, and who trembles at My word.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "To this one I will look, to him who is humble and contrite of spirit, and who trembles at My word" -- after declaring He cannot be housed in a building, God says where He DOES look: toward the broken, the humble, the God-fearing. This is the paradox of 57:15 restated. The God who fills heaven and earth attends to the crushed heart.

As they have chosen their own ways, and their soul delights in their abominations,

verses.flow: Hear the word of the LORD, you who tremble at His word: "Your brothers who hate you, who exclude you for My name's sake, have said, 'Let the LORD be glorified, that we may see your joy.' But they will be put to shame.

verses.flow: A sound of uproar from the city, a voice from the temple, the voice of the LORD who is rendering recompense to His enemies.

verses.flow: "Before she travailed, she brought forth; before her pain came, she gave birth to a boy.
verses.note: ESCHATOLOGICAL: Zion's sudden birth -- "before she travailed, she brought forth" -- is a supernatural event. A nation born in one day (v.8) may refer to Israel's restoration in 1948, or to the church born at Pentecost, or to the eschatological transformation. The imagery emphasizes divine speed: what seems impossible happens instantaneously when God acts.

words.m: particle + verb hophal imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: Who has heard such a thing? Who has seen such things? Can a land be born in one day? Can a nation be brought forth all at once? As soon as Zion travailed, she also brought forth her sons.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 1st common sing

verses.flow: Shall I bring to the point of birth and not give delivery?" says the LORD. "Or shall I who gives delivery shut the womb?" says your God.
