verses.flow: For thus says the LORD: "Behold, I extend peace to her like a river, and the glory of the nations like an overflowing stream; and you will be nursed, you will be carried on the hip and fondled on the knees.

verses.flow: As one whom his mother comforts, so I will comfort you; and you will be comforted in Jerusalem."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "As one whom his mother comforts, so I will comfort you" -- God uses maternal imagery for Himself (cf. 49:15). The comfort of God is not abstract but intimate, physical, like a mother holding a weeping child. This is the God of Isaiah: warrior (63:1-6) AND mother (66:13).

For behold, the LORD will come in fire, and His chariots like the whirlwind, to render His anger with fury, and His rebuke with flames of fire.

verses.flow: "Those who sanctify and purify themselves to go to the gardens, following one in the center, who eat swine's flesh, detestable things and mice, will come to an end altogether," declares the LORD.

verses.flow: "For I know their works and their thoughts; the time is coming to gather all nations and tongues. And they shall come and see My glory.
verses.note: ESCHATOLOGICAL: "The time is coming to gather all nations and tongues" -- the final ingathering of all humanity before God. This is the scene of Revelation 7:9: "a great multitude which no one could count, from every nation and all tribes and peoples and tongues." Isaiah's vision culminates in universal worship.

verses.flow: I will set a sign among them and will send survivors from them to the nations: Tarshish, Put, Lud, Meshech, Rosh, Tubal, and Javan, to the distant coastlands that have neither heard My fame nor seen My glory. And they will declare My glory among the nations.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: God sends survivors to the nations to declare His glory. The list of nations (Tarshish, Put, Lud, Meshech, Tubal, Javan) spans the known world -- from Spain to Africa to Asia Minor. This is the Great Commission in OT form: witnesses sent to the ends of the earth.

verses.flow: Then they shall bring all your brethren from all the nations as a grain offering to the LORD, on horses, in chariots, in litters, on mules and on camels, to My holy mountain Jerusalem," says the LORD, "just as the sons of Israel bring their grain offering in a clean vessel to the house of the LORD.

words.g: to/for a Levite or descendant of Levi

verses.flow: I will also take some of them for priests and for Levites," says the LORD.
"For just as the new heavens and the new earth which I make will endure before Me," declares the LORD, "so your offspring and your name will endure.
verses.note: ESCHATOLOGICAL: "The new heavens and the new earth which I make will endure before Me" -- the new creation is permanent, eternal, unshakable. "So your offspring and your name will endure" -- God's people share in the permanence of the new creation. This is the ultimate promise: eternal existence in a renewed cosmos.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: And it shall be from new moon to new moon and from sabbath to sabbath, all mankind will come to bow down before Me," says the LORD.
verses.note: ESCHATOLOGICAL: "All mankind will come to bow down before Me" -- universal worship, the goal of all history. "From new moon to new moon and from sabbath to sabbath" -- perpetual, rhythmic worship. The sabbath principle endures into eternity: rest, worship, communion with God.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd common plur
conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd common plur
conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd common plur

verses.flow: "Then they will go forth and look on the corpses of the men who have transgressed against Me. For their worm will not die and their fire will not be quenched; and they will be an abhorrence to all mankind."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The book of Isaiah ends with this terrifying verse: "Their worm will not die and their fire will not be quenched" -- Jesus quotes this three times in Mark 9:44-48 to describe Gehenna (hell). The final vision is double-edged: eternal worship (v.23) and eternal judgment (v.24). Isaiah refuses to end on comfort alone. The two destinies stand side by side forever. This is not cruelty but honesty: the God who saves is also the God who judges. The entire book drives toward this choice: turn to Him and be saved (45:22), or face the consequences of rebellion. The last word of Isaiah is "all mankind" (kol basar) -- all flesh will see, and all flesh must choose.
